1: A direct divine speech unit commanding war against Midian. The double speech formula (waydabber YHWH ʾel-Moshe lēmôr) opens a new major section. Midian was the site of the Baal Peor apostasy (Num 25), where Midianite women seduced Israel into idolatry at Balaam's instigation (v16 below), resulting in 24,000 deaths from divine plague.
2: nəqōm niqmat bənê yiśrāʾēl mēʾet hammiḏyānîm — take full vengeance for Israel on the Midianites. The cognate accusative nqm nqmh — take vengeance of vengeance — intensifies the certainty and completeness of the required action. This is divine vengeance executed through Israel, not personal vendetta. The timing note: afterward Moses will be gathered to his people (tiʾāsēp̄ ʾel-ʿammekā) — this campaign is Moses's final act before death (Deut 34).
3: Moses arms men from the people for war: hēḥālēṣû miṭṭikkem ʾănāšîm lassāḇāʾ — draw out men from among you for the host. The verb חָלַץ (ḥālaṣ) — to draw out, equip — connotes selection from the broader community for a specific military purpose. The goal is explicitly stated: laʿăśôt niqmat YHWH — to execute the vengeance of YHWH — YHWH owns the vengeance, Israel are the instruments.
4: One thousand from each tribe (ʾelep̄ lammaṭṭeh ʾelep̄ lammaṭṭeh) — the doubling of the phrase emphasizes the precise proportional levy. The number twelve thousand soldiers (v5) reflects the standard holy-war muster pattern of the tribes contributing equally. The small number — compared to the census figures of 600,000+ — signals this is a symbolic-representative force rather than a total mobilization.
5: wayyimmāsərû mēʾalp̄ê yiśrāʾēl ʾelep̄ lammaṭṭeh — one thousand per tribe were drafted. The verb מָסַר (māsar) — to hand over, deliver — has the connotation of conscription, transferring individuals from community service to military command. The total: twelve thousand armed troops (ḥălûṣê ṣāḇāʾ — equipped for the host).
6: Phinehas son of Eleazar the priest accompanies the army with the holy vessels (kəlê haqqōḏeš) and the signal trumpets (waḥăṣōṣərôt hamtərûʿâ). Phinehas is the priest who stopped the Baal Peor plague by executing the Israelite man and Midianite woman (Num 25:7-8,11). His presence frames this campaign as the completion of the covenant crisis he interrupted.
7: wayyiṣbəʾû ʿal-miḏyān kaʾăšer ṣiwwâ YHWH ʾet-Moshe — they waged war against Midian as YHWH commanded Moses. The war is conducted in explicit obedience to divine command; every action is measured against this standard. wayyahargû kol-zākār — they killed every male. The ḥērem (devoted-destruction) principle applies here even without the word being used: total destruction of combatants.
8: The five Midianite kings are named: Evi, Rekem, Zur, Hur, and Reba — the same five named in Josh 13:21 as vassal kings under Sihon of the Amorites, placing Midian in a tributary relationship with the Transjordanian kings already defeated. בִּלְעָם בֶּן-בְּעוֹר (Bilʿam ben Bəʿôr) — Balaam son of Beor — is also killed. The prophet whom Balak hired to curse Israel (Num 22-24) becomes the instrument of Israel's seduction (v16) and meets his end by the sword.
9: The women, children, livestock, and goods are taken as spoil. The capture rather than killing of the women becomes the point of Moses's furious confrontation in vv14-18. The spoil enumeration follows the standard sequence of human captives and animals, with property following. The scale of the plunder is precisely tabulated in vv32-47.
10: The settled cities (ʿārêhem) and encampments (ṭirôtām) are burned. טִירָה (ṭîrâ) — encampment, unwalled settlement — distinguishes nomadic camp-settlements from fortified cities. Both types of Midianite habitation are destroyed. The thoroughness of the destruction follows the pattern of holy war against devoted peoples (Deut 7:1-5).
11: The army gathers all the plunder — ʾet kol-haššālāl wəʾet kol-hammalqôaḥ bāʾāḏām ûḇabbəhēmâ — both among humans and animals. The distinction šālāl (general plunder/spoil) and malqôaḥ (specifically captured livestock/persons) reflects the formal vocabulary of spoil-distribution law that follows in vv25-47.
12: The army returns to the camp at the plains of Moab by the Jordan across from Jericho — the final encampment position of the wilderness period. The gathered captives and spoil are brought to Moses and Eleazar for judgment. The three-fold authority — Moses (prophetic), Eleazar (priestly), the congregation (communal) — mirrors the judicial structure of ch15's Sabbath-breaker case.
13: Moses, Eleazar, and the leaders go out to meet the returning army. The going-out to meet (wayyēṣəʾû... ligrāʾt) is the standard honor-reception for returning warriors, which makes Moses's subsequent fury (v14) all the more dramatic. He approaches to receive them and instead confronts them.
14: wayyiqṣōp̄ Moshe ʿal pəqûḏê heḥāyil — Moses was furious at the officers of the force. קָצַף (qāṣap̄) — to be furious, wrathful — is used for both divine and human anger; the word appears for YHWH's wrath against Israel (Num 1:53; 17:11; 18:5). Moses's rage mirrors the divine anger that required this campaign in the first place. The officers are challenged: why have you kept all the women alive?
15: hăḥayyîtem kol-nəqēḇâ — you have let all the females live? The interrogative is rhetorical: Moses already knows the answer. The question establishes the warrant for what follows — the women's survival is presented as a failure to complete the divine mandate, specifically in light of what the women did at Peor.
16: The explanatory charge: hēnnâ hāyû liḇnê yiśrāʾēl bidiḇar Bilʿām — these same women were the ones who, following Balaam's counsel, caused Israel to act treacherously against YHWH in the matter of Peor. The phrase biḏbar Bilʿām — according to Balaam's word/counsel — identifies Balaam's strategy: unable to curse Israel directly (Num 22-24), he apparently counseled Moab/Midian to seduce Israel through women (cf. Rev 2:14). The result was the plague in Num 25 (hammageep̱â ʿal-ʿăḏat YHWH).
17: The expanded command: kill every male child (kol-zākār battāp̄) and every woman who has known a man sexually (kol-ʾiššâ yōḏaʿat ʾîš). The phrase יָדַע אִישׁ (yāḏaʿ ʾîš) — known a man — is the standard biblical idiom for sexual intercourse (Gen 4:1,17,25). This expanded command applies the ḥērem logic: those who participated in or could continue the seduction-threat to Israel are killed; those who were not party to it are preserved.
18: The young girls who have not known a man sexually are kept alive. The distinction between combatant-women (those who participated in the Peor seduction as adults) and non-combatant girls follows a logic similar to Deut 20:13-14, which distinguishes between male combatants (killed) and women and children (preserved) in distant-city wars. The Midianite war applies ḥērem to those who specifically acted against Israel at Peor.
19: Purification law for the returning soldiers: anyone who killed (kol hōrēg nep̄eš) or touched a corpse (nōgēaʿ beḥālāl) must remain outside the camp for seven days and purify themselves on the third and seventh days (the standard corpse-impurity purification from Num 19:11-12). Warfare, even divinely commanded warfare, generates corpse-impurity requiring ritual cleansing before re-entry to the holy camp.
20: Garments, leather, goat-hair, and wooden articles must also be purified. The comprehensive list covers all materials that could absorb corpse-impurity through contact. The purification principle extends beyond human persons to inanimate objects, reflecting the understanding that impurity is transmissible through physical contact with any material that has been in the presence of the dead.
21: Eleazar pronounces the ordinance for the captured metal: gold, silver, bronze, iron, tin, and lead. The priest rather than Moses delivers this ruling — perhaps because metal purification involves fire, which relates to priestly jurisdiction over the altar. The ruling is explicitly grounded in Mosaic authority: zōʾt ḥuqqat hattôrâ ʾăšer ṣiwwâ YHWH ʾet-Moshe.
22: The six metals listed — gold, silver, bronze, iron, tin, lead — represent the full spectrum of metals used in the ancient Near East. Their order (from most precious to least) follows standard ancient metallurgical hierarchy. The list is exhaustive by design: every possible metal article is covered by the purification ruling.
23: Items that can withstand fire must be passed through fire and purified with the mê niddâ — the water of impurity (the red-heifer purification water from Num 19). Items that cannot withstand fire must be passed through water only. The dual requirement — fire AND water — for metal items is more stringent than the water-only standard for organic materials, reflecting both the practical durability of metal and its heightened purification requirement as articles from a dedicated people.
24: Clothes are washed on the seventh day. After washing, the soldiers are clean and may enter the camp. The seven-day structure aligns with the standard corpse-impurity timeline (Num 19:11-19), and the entry into the camp on the eighth day (implicitly the day after the seventh) parallels the new-beginnings significance of the eighth day throughout the Torah.
25: A new divine speech unit (vv25-47) legislating the division of the plunder. The legal structure here — with precise fractional levies — exemplifies the P tradition's careful accounting of holy-war spoil as a matter of covenant principle, not merely military practice.
26: Moses, Eleazar, and the tribal leaders are to take a full count (śəʾû ʾet-rōʾš hammalqôaḥ) of all the captured goods — persons and animals. מַלְקוֹחַ (malqôaḥ) — capture/spoil taken by tongs/grasp — is the technical term for items taken in combat as opposed to items purchased or inherited. The count is to be precise and documented before distribution.
27: wəḥāṣîtā ʾet-hammalqôaḥ bên tōp̄əśê hammilyāmâ... ûḇên kol-hāʿēḏâ — divide the plunder equally between the fighters and the whole congregation. The 50-50 split follows the principle that the community that cannot fight equally shares in the victory — the same principle behind Joshua's distribution of Transjordanian plunder to the tribes that stayed behind (Josh 22:8) and David's ordinance after the Ziklag raid (1 Sam 30:24-25).
28: From the warriors' 50%, a tribute to YHWH: one out of every 500 persons, cattle, donkeys, and sheep. מֶכֶס (mekes) — levy, tribute — appears only in this chapter in the Torah, cognate to Akkadian miksu (toll/customs duty). The 1/500 rate (0.2%) is the warriors' tribute because they were the proximate agents of the victory; their tithe is proportionally smaller than the community's.
29: The warriors' tribute is given to Eleazar the priest as a tərûmah laYHWH — a contribution lifted up to YHWH. The category tərûmâ — lifted contribution — connects spoil-distribution to the regular offering system (Lev 7:14; Num 15:19-21), integrating military victory into the same framework of returning the firstfruits to YHWH.
30: From the congregation's 50%, a 1/50 levy (2%) — five times the warriors' rate. This higher proportion reflects that the community received their share without personal risk; they owe more precisely because they gave less. The congregation's levy goes to the Levites (lal·wiyyim šōmərê mišmeret miškan YHWH — the Levites who keep the charge of YHWH's dwelling).
31: wayyaʿaś Moshe wəʾelʿāzār hakkōhēn kaʾăšer ṣiwwâ YHWH ʾet-Moshe — Moses and Eleazar did exactly as YHWH commanded Moses. The compliance formula closes the command-unit; the distribution proceeds by exact divine instruction. The passive voice of the formula — they did as commanded — emphasizes obedience rather than initiative.
32: The spoil count begins. The precision of the figures (six hundred seventy-five thousand sheep/goats) reflects either careful military accounting or the stylized large-number conventions of ancient Near Eastern military records. Scholars debate whether these numbers are literal or symbolic/representative; the text presents them as the actual enumerated result of the divinely commanded levy.
33: Seventy-two thousand cattle. The four categories of plunder — sheep, cattle, donkeys, and persons — follow the standard ancient inventory sequence, moving from the most common livestock to draft animals to human captives.
34: Sixty-one thousand donkeys. חֲמוֹר (ḥamôr) — donkey — was a primary draft and transport animal in the ancient Near East; the number represents significant transportation and agricultural capacity captured from Midian.
35: Thirty-two thousand young women (nep̄eš ʾāḏām minhannāšîm ʾăšer lōʾ yāḏəʿû miškāḇ zākār — persons from among the women who had not known lying with a male). The legal language of v18 is here applied as the criterion for the enumeration: only those not involved in the Peor seduction were preserved. The number is recorded alongside the livestock — not because persons are equated with animals but because the same distribution law applies to all categories of captured property.
36: The warriors' half of the sheep: 337,500. The precise halving of the total confirms the 50-50 distribution principle stated in v27 — the narrator demonstrates the principle was actually applied, not merely proclaimed.
37: The YHWH tribute from the warriors' sheep: 675 (1/500 of 337,500 = 675). The exactness of the calculation demonstrates the precision of the Mosaic law's application; the 1/500 ratio yields a whole number confirming the mathematical coherence of the levy system.
38: From 36,000 cattle in the warriors' half, the YHWH tribute is 72 (exactly 1/500). The consistent precision across all four categories — sheep, cattle, donkeys, persons — establishes the levy as a systematic proportional tax, not an approximation.
39: From 30,500 donkeys in the warriors' half, the YHWH tribute is 61 (exactly 1/500). The fractional precision of these calculations across large numbers suggests either exact accounting or that the figures were set to yield whole-number tithes — a possibility consistent with round-number conventions in ancient military records.
40: From 16,000 persons in the warriors' half, the YHWH tribute is 32 (exactly 1/500). The persons given as tribute to the priest enter the sanctuary system, likely as temple servants (nətînîm), the class of dedicated servants mentioned in Ezra-Nehemiah and attributed to Mosaic origin (Ezra 8:20).
41: Moses transfers the priestly tribute (mekes tərûmat YHWH — the levy as YHWH's contribution) to Eleazar. The compliance formula mirrors v31, closing the warriors' distribution unit. Each stage of the distribution is documented and attributed to explicit divine command.
42: The congregation's half is separated by Moses — hēḇdîl Moshe — from the warriors' share. The formal separation act mirrors the havdalah principle (separation of sacred categories) throughout the Torah: the two halves must not be confused or merged; their different levy rates require distinct accounting.
43: The congregation's half of sheep: 337,500. Identical to the warriors' half, confirming the exact 50-50 split. The repetition of the numbers for each category serves the legal record: the document establishes what each party received, not merely what the total was.
44: The congregation's half of cattle: 36,000. The ledger continues with the same precision as the warriors' half, building a complete written accounting of the distribution — a formal administrative record of covenant compliance.
45: The congregation's half of donkeys: 30,500. As with the warriors' half (v39), the congregation receives exactly the same number of each category.
46: The congregation's half of persons: 16,000. The persons are divided with the same precision as the animals; the distribution follows the same legal category. The Levites receive 320 of these (1/50 of 16,000), matching v30's prescribed rate exactly.
47: wayyiqaḥ Moshe miḥăṣî bənê yiśrāʾēl ʾet-hāʾeḥāḏ ʾāḥuz min-haḥamiššîm — Moses took from the Israelites' half one out of every fifty. The 1/50 from the congregation's portion goes to the Levites, completing the levy structure. The Levites receive more proportionally (2% vs. 0.2%) but from the community's larger base, yielding a larger absolute number — consistent with the Levites' role as the permanent servants of the sanctuary.
48: The military officers — commanders of thousands and hundreds — approach Moses with an unexpected report. Their voluntary approach after the distribution is complete signals that what follows is not a commanded action but an initiative of the officers themselves.
49: The report: every man under their command has been counted (p̄āqaḏnû ʾet-ʿăḇāḏê hammilḥāmâ), and not one man is missing (wəlōʾ-nip̄qaḏ mimmennû ʾîš). Zero battlefield casualties among twelve thousand soldiers in a campaign against five Midianite kings and their forces. The officers recognize this as an extraordinary divine protection.
50: In response to the miraculous preservation, the officers bring a voluntary offering: gold articles — armlets (ʾeṣʿāḏâ), bracelets (ṣāmiḏ), signet rings (ṭabbāʿat), earrings (ʿāgîl), and pendants (kûmāz). The stated purpose: lekappēr ʿal-nap̄šōtênû — to make atonement for our lives/souls. Even without personal combat death, the officers recognize that warfare itself, and perhaps their involvement in the expanded killing of vv17-18, requires atonement.
51: Moses and Eleazar receive the gold — all worked articles (kol-kelî maʿăśeh). The detailed specification of worked gold distinguishes it from raw gold; the articles are identifiable objects carrying cultural and personal histories from the Midianites who wore them, now consecrated to YHWH's purposes.
52: The total: sixteen thousand seven hundred fifty shekels of gold from the officers' voluntary contribution. The precision of the weight — not an approximate round number — reinforces the administrative thoroughness of the record. The large quantity reflects the scale of the Midianite wealth that was captured.
53: ʾanšê hazzāḇāʾ bāzəzû ʾîš lô — the rank-and-file soldiers had each taken spoil for themselves. The officers' voluntary offering is distinguished from personal spoil retained by individual soldiers under the provisions of vv27-47. The distinction between the communal distribution (required) and the officers' voluntary offering (spontaneous) frames the latter as above-and-beyond covenant devotion.
54: Moses and Eleazar bring the gold into the tent of meeting as a zikkārôn liḇnê yiśrāʾēl lip̄nê YHWH — a memorial for the Israelites before YHWH. זִכָּרוֹן (zikkārôn) — memorial/reminder — frames the gold offering not as payment for past service but as a permanent covenant-reminder object. The tent of meeting functions as the repository of covenant memory (cf. the jar of manna, Aaron's rod, the tablets) — objects that embody the history of YHWH's acts among his people.
